entry.title: Joule for Developers ABAP AI Sapphire and ASUG 2026 Sessions
entry.content:
In case you do not have planned your Sapphire Orlando and ASUG agenda here are my recommendations if you want to learn about the latest innovations from ABAP Platform and Joule for developers (J4D) ABAP AI capabilities:

 

  1. ABAP Cloud: Entering the new era of ABAP development | BTP2107: Sonja Lienard (Head of ABAP Platform, SAP SE) will explain ABAP Cloud development model, agentic development experience in VS Code, the SAP Joule for Developers add-on, and agentic custom code migration and the strategy behind the evolution.
  2. Reimagine transformation with migration and modernization assistants | ERP2493: Sonja Lienard (Head of ABAP Platform, SAP SE) and Sören Ruder (Chief Product Officer and Global Head of Customer Experience & Solutions) will explore how SAP agentic AI will accelerate ERP migration and modernization by compressing timelines, and unlock faster time-to-value on your journey to a modern, cloud ERP.
  3. Introduction to ABAP Cloud supported by generative AI | BTP1429: Get an overview on ABAP Cloud and recent innovations, get insights on Clean Core and see how generative AI boosts your adoption of ABAP Cloud conducted by Volker Drees and Nora Klemp (ABAP Platform Product Management).
  4. Build smarter: Agentic ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code | BTP2573: Simona Marincei (Head of AI - SAP ABAP Platform) and Holger Bruchelt (Product Manager, Microsoft Corporation) will show how ABAP agents orchestrate in VS Code via the ABAP MCP-server tasks, speed up coding, and boost productivity across orchestrators from various partners.
  5. Agentic ABAP with SAP Joule for Developers: Build faster, migrate smarter | JOU1428: Simona Marincei (Head of AI - SAP ABAP Platform), Holger Bruchelt (Product Manager, Microsoft Corporation) and Nora Klemp (Senior Product Specialist) will explain how agentic ABAP accelerates SAP S/4HANA migrations and streamline ABAP Cloud development across modern and legacy environments.
  6. Customer Influence and feedback: The product road map for ABAP Cloud | BTP1603: Learn from Vanessa Micelli-Schmidt (VP, Head of ABAP Platform Experience, SAP SE) how to give feedback to the ABAP product roadmap with the Customer Influence site which allows submitting, tracking, and prioritizing ideas.
  7. Modernize classic extensions for clean core in SAP Cloud ERP Private | BTP1426: Volker Drees (Product Expert, ABAP Cloud, SAP SE) will explain clean core extensibility practice in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Learn how to govern clean core development using the ABAP test cockpit and how to use the ABAP Cloud development model and clean core extensibility principles to modernize your custom on-stack extensions.

 

The full Sapphire Orlando and ASUG 2026 (11.05.2026 - 13.05.2026) agenda can be found here...
...and here is the agenda for Sapphire Madrid (19.05.2026 - 21.05.26).
 
Here you can learn more:
  1. Our Roadmap for 2026 before Sapphire
  2. Your Roadmap for getting started with ABAP AI
  3. ABAP AI Revolution Accelerates: The ABAP Developer Who Built Enterprise Apps in Minutes
  4. Joule for Developers and ABAP AI capabilities are coming to SAP S/4HANA Private Edition 2021, 2022, and 2023
  5. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  6. Help & Guides
    1. Step-by-step guide how to activate SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
    2. Joule for Developers (J4D) for S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup Guide
    3. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    4. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    5. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    6. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    7. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    8. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    9. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    10. ABAP AI SDK Help
    11. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here . Here you find more on ABAP- 1,

entry.title: How I Connected Claude AI to My SAP ABAP System Using MCP — A Complete Windows Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP developer community has been buzzing about connecting Claude AI directly to live SAP ABAP systems using MCP (Model Context Protocol) — reading code, modifying objects, creating transports, and activating changes, all from a natural language chat interface.

I tried it myself on Windows, and it works. This blog walks you through exactly how to set it up.

What is MCP?

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets AI models like Claude connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a plugin system for AI — instead of just answering questions from training data, Claude can actually call tools that interact with real systems.

For SAP developers, this means Claude can:

  • Read your actual ABAP code directly from your system
  • Write and modify objects
  • Run syntax checks
  • Create transport requests
  • Activate objects

All without you copy-pasting anything.

The Architecture

 
 
Claude Desktop / Claude Code CLI         ↕ MCP Protocol  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │   mcp-abap-adt           │  ← Read-only (13 tools)  │   mcp-abap-abap-adt-api  │  ← Full CRUD (30 tools)  └──────────────────────────┘         ↕ ADT REST API    Your SAP ABAP System

Both MCP servers were built by Mario Andreschak and communicate with SAP via the same ADT (ABAP Development Tools) API that Eclipse uses under the hood.

Prerequisites

Before starting, you need:

  • Windows 10 or 11 (64-bit)
  • An SAP ABAP system with /sap/bc/adt active in SICF
  • A user with SAP_ADT_DEVELOPER role (or equivalent)
  • A Claude Pro/Max/Team subscription at claude.ai
  • Internet connection

Step 1 — Install Node.js

Node.js is the runtime required by Claude Code CLI and the MCP servers.

  1. Go to https://nodejs.org and download the LTS version
  2. Install with all defaults
  3. Verify in Command Prompt:
 
 
node -v    → v24.x.xnpm -v     → 11.x.x

Step 2 — Install Git for Windows

Git provides the bash shell Claude Code requires on Windows.

  1. Go to https://git-scm.com/downloads/win and download the installer
  2. During setup, on the PATH screen select: "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software"
  3. Complete with all other defaults

Step 3 — Install Claude Code CLI

 
 
cmd
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-codeclaude --version

Then log in:

 
 
cmd
claude

Select option 1 (Claude subscription), log in via browser, return to terminal.

Step 4 — Install mcp-abap-adt (Read-Only MCP Server)

This server gives Claude read access to your SAP system with 13 tools including GetProgram, GetClass, GetFunction, GetTable, SearchObject, and more.

 
 
cmd
mkdir C:\Toolscd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-adtcd mcp-abap-adtnpm installnpm run build

Create a .env file in the folder:

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USERNAME=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register with Claude Code:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-adt/dist/index.jsclaude mcp list

You should see: mcp-abap-adt: ✓ Connected

Step 5 — Install mcp-abap-abap-adt-api (Full CRUD Server)

This is the powerful one — 30 tools including read, write, syntax check, activate, lock/unlock, and transport management.

 
 
cmd
cd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api.gitcd mcp-abap-abap-adt-apinpm installnpm run build

Create .env (note: slightly different variable names):

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USER=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300SAP_LANGUAGE=ENNODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt-api node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api/dist/index.js

Step 6 — Setup Claude Desktop (GUI Interface)

Claude Desktop is where it really comes alive — a beautiful chat UI like the LinkedIn video.

  1. Download from https://claude.ai/download
  2. Install and log in
  3. Go to ☰ → Settings → Developer → Edit Config
  4. Replace the content with:
 
 
json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "mcp-abap-adt": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-adt\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USERNAME": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    },
    "mcp-abap-adt-api": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-abap-adt-api\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USER": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "SAP_LANGUAGE": "EN",
        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    }
  }
}

⚠️ Windows-specific tip: You MUST use the full path C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe — using just "node" does not work in Claude Desktop on Windows. Also note double backslashes \\ in all paths.

  1. Kill Claude in Task Manager and reopen
  2. Go back to Settings → Developer — you should see both servers listed as running ✅

Step 7 — Add Free Official SAP MCP Servers

Bonus: Claude Desktop has a built-in directory of official SAP MCP servers. Click "Connect your tools to Claude" at the bottom of the chat, search for "SAP", and add:

  • SAPUI5 MCP Server — for UI5 development
  • SAP CAP MCP Server — for CAP projects
  • SAP Fiori MCP Server — for Fiori Elements apps

Also add these free hosted servers to Claude Code CLI (no installation needed):

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add --transport http abap-docs https://mcp-abap.marianzeis.de/mcpclaude mcp add --transport http sap-docs https://mcp-sap-docs.marianzeis.de/mcp

These give Claude access to the full ABAP keyword documentation across 8 SAP releases, ABAP cheat sheets, CAP docs, UI5 docs, and SAP Community posts.

Testing It

Open Claude Desktop, create a New Chat, and type:

 
 
Get the source code of program SAPMV45A

Watch Claude automatically call your SAP system, retrieve the source code, and explain it — all in seconds.

For write operations (test on $TMP objects only!):

 
 
Find a simple Z program in $TMP, add a comment at the top saying "Modified by Claude", save and activate it

For complex workflows like the LinkedIn video:

 
 
Connect to my SAP system. Copy class ZCL_SOURCE from package ZPKG_A into ZPKG_B, rename it to ZCL_TARGET, change the carrier_id in the SELECT for /DMO/CONNECTION, create a transport and activate.

Security Notes

  • Never commit .env files to Git
  • Change your SAP password if shared in screenshots
  • Use dedicated service accounts with minimum required authorizations
  • Only test write operations in $TMP before moving to real packages
  • The TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 flag disables SSL validation — use only in dev environments
  • Check your company's AI policy before sending code to external APIs

My Experience

What surprised me most is how Claude handles the entire workflow autonomously. It doesn't just write code — it searches for the object, reads the source, understands the structure, reads class includes, creates the transport, locks the object, writes the code, runs a syntax check, activates, and unlocks. All from one natural language request.

 

Important Hint

It can make ABAP-specific mistakes (like forgetting RAP locking fields, or using reserved DDIC words). Always review AI-generated code before activating in production. But as a development accelerator and code reviewer, it's genuinely powerful.

References

  • GitHub: mcp-abap-adt — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: mcp-abap-abap-adt-api — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: sap-skills — 35 Claude Code plugins for SAP
  • Blog: SAP Docs MCP Server — Marian Zeis
  • Blog: Claude Code via MCP — SAP Community
  • Blog: Installing and Extending an ABAP MCP Server — SAP Community

I hope this helps other ABAP developers get started. If you run into issues or have questions, feel free to comment below. Happy coding!

entry.title: The Quiet Revolution in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Logistics: Clean Core, Transparency, and AI
entry.content:
Supply chains are no longer just about moving physical goods; they are highly synchronized, data-driven ecosystems. As we navigate the latest updates in SAP S/4HANA—particularly with the "progress without disruption" philosophy of the 2025 releases—it is clear that the focus has shifted. We are moving away from reactive workarounds and toward proactive, intelligent, and strictly governed logistics.

For those of us working deep in the trenches of Sourcing, Procurement, and Warehouse Management, this shift brings both exciting capabilities and new operational challenges. Here is a look at the three pillars redefining our day-to-day logistics architecture in the Public Cloud.

1. The Discipline of Clean Core Extensibility

Historically, enforcing specific business rules—like making the "Requisitioner" or "Purchasing Organization" fields mandatory on a Purchase Requisition—often led to messy backend modifications. Today, the Public Cloud enforces a much-needed discipline.

Standard customizing has its limits, but leveraging Key User Extensibility is proving to be a game-changer. By utilizing specific Custom Logic (BAdIs) for Field Control (MM_PUR_S4_PR_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE) and Item Checks (MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK), we can enforce strict data quality right at the entry point of procurement. This approach ensures compliance without compromising the clean core, guaranteeing that our systems remain upgrade-safe and resilient.

2. The Push for Total Process Transparency

True efficiency requires total visibility, yet there are still standard gaps we need to address as a community. A perfect example is workflow simulation.

With release 2602, SAP introduced the highly anticipated ability to simulate workflows for Purchase Orders, allowing teams to validate conditions before documents are processed. However, this same transparency is critically missing for Purchase Requisitions. PRs are the genesis of the procurement lifecycle. Without simulation capabilities at the PR level, organizations are left relying on reactive troubleshooting and trial-and-error. Pushing for these types of intuitive enhancements (like supporting IR #365219) is vital for maintaining process consistency from end to end.

3. Smarter Warehousing and System Resilience

Execution is where strategy meets reality. In the Warehouse Management space, the transition to Public Cloud highlights the need for advanced, automated housekeeping. While traditional EWM robustly handles rearrangement and slotting, Public Cloud WM environments still require creative approaches or scheduled optimization jobs to handle routine bin cleanups and stock consolidation effectively.

As we look toward the future of smart logistics—where predictive maintenance data and real-time equipment health will directly trigger warehouse and procurement tasks—backend stability is non-negotiable. SAP’s recent moves, such as introducing Multi-Availability Zones for the BTP ABAP environment and offering scalable 0.5 ACU runtimes for agile development, provide the exact infrastructure needed to build these high-volume, highly resilient integrations.

Bridging the Gap with AI

Finally, the tools to build this future are evolving faster than ever. From leveraging AI-powered UI5 plugins for rapid application development to integrating Joule into the end-user experience, the technology is ready. The remaining barrier is no longer software; it is the skills gap. As an ecosystem, our next major hurdle is moving past the hype and focusing on the tactical, day-one skills required to actually deploy these AI tools in live logistics environments.

What are your thoughts? Are you finding that Clean Core extensibility is fully meeting your complex procurement needs, or are there still gaps in standard Public Cloud WM functionality that your teams are struggling to work around? Let’s discuss in the comments below!

entry.title: Demo Scenario: Clean Core Extensibility with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build
entry.content:
Co-Authors: @Sivakumar_Subramaniam , @ShivaniShankar 

A common feedback when talking about clean core extensibility is the absence of demo-ready content to visualize these guidelines that have evolved over time. This blog post attempts to take an extensibility scenario as example and showcase the equivalent scenario in a clean-core compliant world. Nevertheless, we cannot claim to be covering every possible flow in one post. The intent is to showcase one example covering a wide range. In the process, we walk through some of the decisions taken that go beyond technology and consider business aspects as well. A lot of technical details are omitted since this post is already quite long. Look forward to the discussions and feedback in the comments section to assess interest and demand for similar posts/more technical versions. 

Recommended Pre-read: 

While an effort is made to abstract technical complexity to a great extent, a reader still needs some basic understanding of the SAP ecosystem, how extensions work, why they exist and some background of clean core.

To maximize takeaways from this blog post, consider reading the clean core extensibility whitepaper before you start, particularly if "Clean Core" sounds unfamiliar. Due to the rich context, answers to why clean core etc available in the whitepaper already, we skip it here. Nevertheless, a lot of what this blog post covers would not make much sense without this background/context. 

Key Assumptions: 

1. We consider level-A of clean core extensibility since this is the highest standard. If one were to target level-B for example, the target architecture might look a little different with potential reuse of certain objects

2. For simplicity and to keep the focus on extensibility, we assume there is no fit-to-standard solution available for this scenario which may or may not be factually correct. In a real project, please consider fit-to-standard analysis and an analysis of your existing custom-developed artefacts before developing something new

Scenario Description : 
- Provide a capability to add custom gift cards when creating sales orders in SAP Cloud ERP Private (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition)

- On applying the gift card, a discount is added to the sales order by an amount equal to the gift card amount

- On creation of a sales order, loyalty points need to get accrued against the membership of the business partner (from the sales order). For simplicity, assume only business partners of type "Customer"

- The loyalty program has tiers and based on the tier the customer is on, he/she/they accrue a percentage of the sales order value as points

- It shall be possible for a customer to request an upgrade to their loyalty program tier. In this case, approval from an admin is required before the change is active 

Scenario Refinement:  

Understanding/context: When working with sales orders, this company has 2 parallel reward programs. One, a gift card that can be applied on creation of the sales order for an immediate discount. Another, a loyalty program where one accrues points with each sales order. Potentially, for redemption (not in scope here) through different means at a later point in time

Tasks:

- Since gift cards need to be applied, there is also an unsaid need to be able to create and maintain these gift cards (by an admin). This means an app to create/maintain gift cards with appropriate fields

- To be able to apply the gift card when creating a sales order, an option is needed directly in the sales order application (or transaction)

- The applied gift card should impact the value of the sales order before creation (not post-processing) 

- Similarly, loyalty program tiers would need to be maintained (possibly as business configuration)

- The loyalty points are calculated based on the created sales order and don't have a dependency or need to update/modify the consistency of the sales order itself. This could be handled as a separate activity from the creation of the sales order

Solution Architecture (Classic Extensibility - non-Clean Core level-A):In the classic extensibility scenario which is non-clean core level-A, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - WebDynPro Application
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in VA01 (Transaction code for sales order creation) - User Exit
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Module Pool Program
  4. Tier upgrade - Classic workflow

 Solution Architecture (with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build - Clean Core)

In the clean-core level-A compliant extensibility scenario, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - Application developed using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) with on-stack developer extensibility. Yes! with real code written by a developer inside the S/4HANA system outside of key user tools and still the highest level of clean core! If this is against your understanding of clean core, read SAP's official whitepaper (section 3.2 at the time this blog post is written) linked in the pre-read section of this blog post!  
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in the standard sales order Fiori app  - Key User Extensibility
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Side-by-side extension using BTP ABAP Environment
  4. Maintenance of tiers of the loyalty program - Business Configuration in the BTP ABAP Environment (not shown in the demo except as value help)
  5. Tier upgrade - SAP Build Process Automation
  6. Communication between S/4HANA and BTP - Event Mesh (or Advanced Event Mesh)

Alternate options - The Loyalty Points Application could also have been developed side-by-side using CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) with additional handling for tier maintenance. 

Decision Criteria (Technical and Business): 

  1. The use case around application of gift card has a very tight dependency on the sales order. The standard SAP sales order application needs to understand and bring up the gift cards for a value help, have the gift cards applied consistently across impacted business objects when the sales order is updated. All of this needs to happen as part of the sales order creation/update process in S/4HANA. This makes on-stack developer extensibility and key-user extensibility good candidates. 
  2. The loyalty points application has 2 factors to consider - Side-by-side v/s on-stack and CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
    • Side-by-Side v/s On-Stack:
      • Decision: Side-by-Side
      • In private cloud/on-premise, SAP ships a new major version every 2 years. By moving out of the core (S/4HANA) anything that can be moved logically without adverse performance/over-engineering, etc, the customer can benefit from always being on the latest release and getting the latest updates. This de-risks innovation from upgrade project timelines and upgrade strategy. 
      • Enforcement of clean core - With the side-by-side approach, clean core is enforced with syntax checks etc since ABAP Cloud is the only option on BTP ABAP Environment as opposed to governance based approaches on-stack which may need an ABAP Cloud package and processes to ensure a developer doesn't slip back to the familiar classic extensibility when cloud ready alternatives are available.
    • CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
      • Decision: Both options are valid. Here, ABAP Cloud due to developer skill set and business configuration handling know-how. Since each option has strengths and benefits, refer the BTP Developer's guide for details. To generalize any of the two as always the better option without case-to-case analysis and comparison may be an unfair/sub-optimal decision. 

General question on BTP ABAP Environment cost -
"The cost of BTP ABAP Environment is higher" - Apart from the smaller runtime size now available, which reduces costs, the cost needs to be spread out across applications being developed. One instance of the BTP ABAP Environment can be reused for multiple applications so long as the runtime/memory requirements are within available limits. Compare this to additional costs for CF Runtime (for example) for each deployed app with CAP and the costing at solution level at scale tends to be similar. This is not to say CAP is a bad option, just that when the decision is influenced by cost, how this cost is calculated decides quite a bit! When this becomes equivalent, the models can be compared on best fit for the use case on technical/architectural grounds.

Demo of functional scenario in both approaches along with ATC findings across levels - B, C, D on the package where classic extensibility was used

The following demo showcases the scenario with classic extensibility as well as with Clean Core Level-A. It also touches upon ATC checks on the package containing the classic extension. To understand how ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) helps categorize the findings, refer section 3.3.4(at the time of writing) of the clean core extensibility whitepaper (in the pre-read section)

 

Call to Action: Comment below what you would wish was better covered in the post and what key takeaways you had! Your inputs help us understand and validate where such blog posts can add more value

entry.title: Business partner Master data change history
entry.content:
Introduction:- 

In modern SAP systems, the concept of the Business Partner (BP) plays a central and combining role in managing master data. SAP introduced the Business Partner approach to simplify data maintenance and improve consistency across applications.  
 
A Business Partner in SAP S/4HANA represents any Person, organization, or group with whom a company conducts business. This includes customers, vendors, employees, and other . SAP puts all roles into one place, reducing duplicates and keeping data accurate. 

One important thing about this Business Partner management is change history tracking. In real-world business scenarios, master data is frequently updated—such as address changes, bank details, or role assignments.   
 
In this blog, we will explore why change history may not appear and what causes it, using simple examples. 

Things You Should Know About Change History in Business Partner:- 

When you need to track changes made to a business partner, you can use the change history feature to view and display those updates clearly. 

We use two tables for change history: CDHDR (Change History Header) and CDPOS (Change History Item). 

In CDHDR, you need to pass the business partner number in the Object ID field, which will display all the related changes. 

In this blog, we will explore the various reasons why change history may not appear in the business partner (BP), using simple and practical examples.

Create a business partner (BP) and make some changes to it. In this example, I have updated the Search Term field from #128 to #127. 

You can check the change history in a business partner (BP) in two ways: 
Extras → Change History → For This Partner: 

This displays all the changes that have been made to the business partner. 
Extras → Change History → For This Field: 

This displays the changes related to a specific field only. 

Extras → Change History → For This Partner: 
This displays the change history as shown below. Since we only updated the Search Term field, it shows changes related to that field only. 

Sometimes, even after making changes, they are not captured in the change history, and no records are stored in the CDHDR or CDPOS tables. 

First, check the BUT000 (General Data of Business Partner) table for the field where changes are not being recorded. Then, verify whether the “Change Document” option is enabled in the data element, as shown below. Make sure this option is active so that change history can be stored properly. 

Second, you need to check the field name in the BP transaction using F1 help and then go to Technical Information. This helps you identify the correct technical field details. 

Then, verify the corresponding field name in the BUT000 table. 

Then, check the field group for that field. Click on the field name and, in the T-code search bar, enter BDT_ANALYZER and press enter . 

Then identify the field group for NAME_ORG1 as follows: 
Sections → BUP010 - Name → BUP200 - Organization → Field Groups → 0045 - Organization: Name 1 
So, the field group for NAME_ORG1 is 0045. 

Now go to transaction BUS11 and check whether field group 45 is maintained for NAME_ORG1. This ensures that the field can be fetched through the BAPI and displayed in the output.
 
Sometimes, the field is not maintained in the BUS11 transaction, and in such cases, the change history will not be retrieved for that field, so it will not appear in the output. 

Thirdly, go to transaction BUS7 (Display View "Events": Overview). 

  • Select the CHGD1 event and navigate to Events → Function Module Allocation to view the assigned function modules.  
  • For function modules BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD1 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD1 (Applications BUA and BUB), ensure that the “Call” column is marked with ‘X’.  
  • Navigate back (F3).  

Repeat the same steps for the events CHGD2, CHGD3, and CHGD4, and verify the corresponding function modules: 

  • CHGD2 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD2 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD2  
  • CHGD3 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD3 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD3  
  • CHGD4 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD4 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD4  
  • Finally, save the changes on the screen . 

We have the report BUSCHDOC to view the change history for multiple business partners. Using this report, we can display change history for a large number of BPs efficiently. 

Additionally, the BPCD transaction is available for the BUSCHDOC report. It generates the selection screen based on the application object and provides a multi-screen report to analyze business partner change documents. 

Prerequisites to execute BUSCHDOC report:- 
Sometimes in customer systems, the BUSCHDOC report may not display the output screen. To make the output visible, you need to ensure the following: 

BUPSELG5 and BUPSELG0 reports are used to generate the select options for the reports BUSCHDOC for a given application object (Eg: BUPA). 

Execution of these reports is a prerequisite for execution of BUSCHDOC  reports. First report BUPSELG5 should be executed then BUPSELG0. 

Please note these reports are language specific and must be executed once for each logon language. 

Many times, we may encounter a “TIME_OUT” dump while executing this report, especially when processing millions of business partners to display change history. 

Resolution:- 
Business Partner is the leading object in S/4 systems, and the number of applications consuming BP has been increasing with each service pack release. To determine BP changes, all registered applications must be called, and their responses consolidated to generate the output. As the number of applications grows, the processing time also increases. 

Since running BUSCHDOC for all BPs is not a regular daily activity, it is recommended to split the range into variants and execute the report as a background job. 

The second approach is to split the process into parallel jobs instead of running a single job with multiple variants, which can help reduce the overall execution time. However, in this case, you will need to consolidate the outputs from all job logs. 

Simple example to use BUSCHDOC report:- 
This is the BUSCHDOC report output screen. Here, we need to enter BUPA as the application object. 

While executing the BUSCHDOC report in the foreground, it is mandatory to enter the Business Partner number. 

Since entering the Business Partner number is mandatory, we are passing 154-12071. 

If no Business Partner number is entered, the system will display the following popup. Therefore, it is important to always provide a BP range to avoid a runtime dump. 

Output: Change history for BP 154-12071.

Conclusion:- 
In my project, validating SAP Business Partner change document settings improved data transparency and helped quickly identify missing logs using BUS7, BUS11, and BUSCHDOC (BPCD). This reduced troubleshooting effort and sped up issue resolution.

Handling performance with background runs and BP ranges avoided timeouts and ensured smooth processing. Overall, it made monitoring and support more efficient.

If you’ve encountered similar situations or have different approaches, feel free to share your thoughts or ask questions in the comments!

entry.title: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic!
entry.content:
Summary

  • SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition in all releases as of 2021 with planned start in Q2/2026 - update: New ABAP AI offering is live! See details below
  • SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be priced on a consumption basis via AI Units - update: New commercial model is live
  • ABAP Development Tools for VS Code are planned to be GA in Q2/2026  - update: ADT for VS Code is live! See details below.
  • An ABAP MCP Server will be shipped in ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and ABAP Development Tools for VS Code as of Q2/2026 enabling agentic ABAP development experience - update: MCP Server is live!
  • An ABAP Custom Code Transformation agent focusing on the migration from SAP ECC towards SAP S/4HANA is planned for Q2/2026 - update: the first custom code migration agent is live

Intro

Since the start of ABAP AI in early 2025 (“Joule speaks ABAP!”), the update in September 2025 (“ABAP AI – Chapter 2”), now it is time to deliver on the promises made at SAP TechEd 2025 (“Introducing the next era of ABAP development”) – time for chapter 3!

We are entering the agentic era of ABAP development: let's get started.

SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities in lower releases

As of June 2026, Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities are offered through a running on SAP BTP. This hub service combines authentication, license check, access to SAP BTP AI Core and use case specific business logic and can be plugged directly to the developer’s IDE. This way, we can offer ABAP AI capabilities mostly independent of the ABAP backend release. This enables us to serve SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition on releases 2021 and higher with Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26 - update: is live!
  • Supported releases: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2021 and higher
  • Costs: See below
  • Configuration guide and further information see this blog post.

New commercial approach for ABAP AI

The free promotion for J4D ABAP AI capabilities comes to an end in September 2026. Accordingly, it is now time to launch the commercial model: Based on AI units, ABAP AI capabilities are now priced purely based on consumption. This approach combines transparency regarding the real costs based on the usage and is well-suited for dynamic and individual agentic AI use cases.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26 - update: is live!
  • Materials: 8019164 (customers) and 8019670 (partners)
  • Information on how to purchase and activate is provided here

ABAP Development Tools for VS Code

14 years after the launch of ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, time has come to support a new IDE. As Q2 2026, ABAP Development Tools will be available for VS Code. Building on the ABAP Language Server (see “Behind the Design…”), coupled with an ABAP MCP Server, this is the enabler for a truly agentic ABAP development experience, boosting development efficiency to the next level. It sets the foundation to leverage modern coding agents like GitHub Copilot directly for ABAP development. While we start with a small set of development objects and IDE tools focusing on SAP Fiori App development based on the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), we will very soon enhance the scope of object types to serve high priority development scenarios.

The VS Code extension is

  • planned to be available as of Q2/26 - update: is available!
  • Supported releases: release 7.31 and higher
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Costs: free of charge
  • Precise info on how to obtain and get started in this blog post.
  • FAQ
  • Documentation
  • Tutorial

MCP Support for ABAP development

The Language Server provides the required abstraction to gain independence of the IDE sitting on top. A major component of this delivery is the MCP server for ABAP development. It provides powerful tools which help coding agents to perform complex ABAP coding tasks. While the Language Server is currently delivered as part of the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and for VS Code, a potential standalone-shipment of the Language and MCP Server is under discussion.

  • Release planned for Q2/26 - update: is available!
  • Supported in Eclipse and VS Code.
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Supported for release 7.31 and higher
  • Read this blog post to learn how to use the MCP server in VS Code and this post for Eclipse
  • Documentation

Agentic custom code migration for the SAP S/4HANA transformation

Bringing all pieces together, we are launching the first ABAP custom code agent assisting in the transformation of custom code from ECC towards SAP S/4HANA. The required capabilities are offered through the ABAP Language and MCP Server explained above and can be used leveraging a coding agent, for example Amazon Q or GitHub Copilot. Currently, Eclipse must be used for this agent, until all required ABAP object types are supported in VS Code (see above). This agent boosts our customers on the RISE journey when migrating their classic landscape to the Cloud ERP.

  • Availability planned for Q2/26. - update: is available!
  • Supported in Eclipse
  • Prerequisites: Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities via the new commercial approach with AI units (see above) and coding agent on top (e.g. Amazon Q or Github Copilot)
  • Read this blog post for the full information and setup guide
  • Teaser video
  • MCP server configuration in ADT for Eclipse is described here

 

Finally - please have a look at our FAQ document for frequent questions and watch out for the GA releases of these deliverables. We will keep you posted.

Get yourself ready to dive into agentic ABAP development! 😎

entry.title: Announcement: Register to the new CEI project for SAP Application Interface Framework
entry.content:
SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) is an integral part of SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud for implementing, monitoring, and managing application interfaces.

In production environments, when a large number of interface messages fail, manual error diagnosis and error resolution can lead to significant effort and time. To address this, we plan to enhance SAP Application Interface Framework to support an AI-assisted error remediation framework that transforms error handling from tedious manual work into intelligent, automated recovery.

To gather feedback on those planned product improvements, we will run the Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) project AI-Assisted Error Remediation for SAP Application Interface Framework. The CEI project aims to collect feedback on the concept and a first proof of concept at an early stage, focusing on:

  • Error classification: How AI-driven error classification can reduce manual effort.
  • Remediation automation: Validating the remediation approach against real customer scenarios.
  • Autonomous vs. guided remediation: Understanding customer preferences for automatic execution versus human-in-the-loop approval.

If you like to join the upcoming SAP Application Interface Framework influence project, please register here (link leads to the customer influence page).

Registration is open from May 18th until June 21st 2026.

Registration is closed!

Once, the registration closes, we will invite you for an initial call where we go through the scope and the prerequisites of the feedback project. This will be around end of June, begin of July, the actual feedback sessions will start in July.

We are looking forward to your active participation and valuable feedback.

entry.title: How to Send Email for Maintenance Orders Generated by Maintenance Plans in S/4HANA Cloud 2602
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Cloud 2602, there are multiple ways to trigger email notifications when preventive maintenance orders are generated from maintenance plan.

This blog outlines two approaches: one configuration driven (recommended) and one extensibility driven to help you choose the right solution based on your requirements and Clean Core strategy.

Business Requirement

Alert relevant users to their company email address when a maintenance order is generated from a maintenance plan so that they can review and take action promptly.

Option 1: Flexible Workflow for Maintenance Orders (Recommended)

The standard and Clean Core–aligned approach is to use the Fiori app:

F4989 – Flexible Workflow for Maintenance Orders:

🔧Configuration Steps

  • Define Global Preconditions Set workflow trigger conditions such as:
    • Order Type (Preventive)
    • Maintenance Plan ≠ empty (ensures trigger from IP10/IP30)
    • Planning Plant / Planner Group (optional filters)
  • Add Workflow Step
    • Step Type: Notification Only
    • Recipients:
      • Partner on order
      • Planner group
      • Person responsible
      • Specific users
    • Execution:
      • Enable auto-completion in My Inbox
  • Email Notification Setup:
    • Use Fiori app: Manage Email Templates
    • Copy standard template: SWT_CRT_NOTIFY_RECIPIENTS
    • Follow naming convention: YY_02000019_CRT_10
    • Maintain template content:
      • Subject: Alert: Maintenance Order {{OrderNumber}} from PM is generated
      • Body: Maintenance Order {{OrderNumber}} has been generated via Maintenance Plan for Technical Object {{TechnicalObject}}. Please review your technical log.

Prerequisites:

  • User email maintained in:
    • Maintain Business Users
    • Manage Business Partners
  • Preventive order type:
    • Assigned to a Phase Model Profile
    • Workflow trigger based on status change (e.g., Approval)

Key Benefits

  • Fully aligned with SAP Clean Core principles
  • Configuration-driven (no coding required)
  • Business-maintainable and audit-ready
  • Upgrade-safe and scalable

Option 2: BAdI-Based Enhancement (Advanced Scenario)

For scenarios requiring more advanced logic, you can use ABAP Cloud extensibility.

🔧Technical Approach

Implement the cloud-released BAdI: BADI_EAM_MO_CH_BEF_SAVE

Trigger Logic:

  • Execute logic when:
    • Order Type = Preventive
    • Maintenance Plan ≠ empty (indicates IP10/IP30 origin)
    • Optional filters:
      • Planning Plant
      • Planner Group

Email Implementation

  • Use modern email API: CL_BCS_MAIL_MESSAGE

Key Implementation Steps

  • Set sender: .set_sender()
  • Add recipients dynamically: .add_recipient() (e.g., fetch from I_MaintenanceOrder CDS view)
  • Define subject: .set_subject()
  • Send email asynchronously: .send_asynchronous()
  • Helps avoid transactional impact or rollback issues

Key Benefits

  • Full flexibility in:
    • Additional Custom logic as per business needs
    • Recipient determination
    • Email content

Considerations

  • Requires ABAP Cloud (Tier 1 extensibility)
  • Higher development and maintenance effort
  • Needs governance to stay compliant with Clean Core strategy

Recommendation

  • Use Flexible Workflow (Option 1) as the default approach
  • Consider BAdI (Option 2) only when:
    • Workflow limitations prevent required logic
    • Advanced recipient or filtering logic is needed

Final Thoughts

  • For most use cases, Flexible Workflow provides a robust, scalable, and upgrade-safe solution aligned with SAP’s Clean Core strategy.
  • BAdI-based enhancement should be used selectively for advanced scenarios where additional flexibility is required.

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Working Smarter with Date Functions in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and BTP ABAP Environment (Steampunk)
entry.content:
Introduction

If you've ever hardcoded a date in a SELECT statement or asked a user to manually key in a date range every time they run a report, you'll appreciate what Date Functions bring to the table.

Date Functions in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and the SAP BTP ABAP Environment (Steampunk) let you define dynamic, human-readable date definitions — think FirstDayOfCurrentMonth or LastDayOfCurrentYear or CURRENT_QUARTER  or Next30DaysFromToday orTOMORROW, CURRENT_QUARTER, or LAST_FISCAL_YEAR — that automatically resolve to the correct calendar values at runtime. No hardcoding. No manual input. Just clean, reusable date logic that works across analytical queries, ABAP code, and CDS views.

In this post, I'll walk through what Date Functions are, who uses them, and how developers and key users can leverage them across the stack.

What Are Date Functions?

A Date Function is a named, dynamic date or date range definition. Instead of storing a fixed date, you define a rule — and the system calculates the actual value at runtime based on the current system date.

 

Supported Combinations

SAP supports three types of Date Function definitions:

  • Single Date — A dynamic single-date definition. For example, a Date Function defined as TOMORROW will always return the date for tomorrow. If today is 21st May 2026, the function returns 22nd May 2026 — automatically, every time.
  • Relative to Base Date Range — A date range defined with an offset (days, weeks, months, quarters, or years) relative to a single dynamic base date.
  • Configured Range — A date range with both a dynamic start date and a dynamic end date, giving full flexibility for complex time window definitions.

SAP has delivered more than 40 predefined Date Functions out of the box, and the Manage Date Functions app has been available to key users since 2017.

The Three Personas — Who Uses Date Functions and How

Date Functions are designed to serve three distinct personas, each interacting with them differently.

1. Key User — Define and Manage

The Key User is the starting point. Using the Manage Date Functions application, key users can:

  • Browse and explore SAP's 40+ predefined Date Functions
  • Create custom Date Functions tailored to their business calendar or reporting needs
  • Simulate the dynamic values a Date Function will return, before it goes live
  • Transport Date Functions from development through to quality and production systems

This is a no-code capability — no developer involvement needed to define or adjust Date Functions. It puts time-intelligence directly in the hands of functional consultants and power users.

 

2. Developer — Use in CDS Views

The Developer persona integrates Date Functions directly into CDS view source code.

A developer can declare a Date Function parameter in a CDS view with a default value. This makes the CDS view inherently time-aware — the date range it operates on is no longer hardcoded but driven by a named, maintainable definition.

Key users can then further customise Date Function parameters through apps like Custom Analytical Queries, without touching the underlying code.

 

Coming soon: SAP is also working on enabling developers to define Date Functions directly within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) — using the same development environment as CDS views, with a form-based UI and simulate/test capabilities built in. This is currently in progress (WIP) and subject to change, but it will further unify the developer experience.

3. Business User — Consume in Analytical Reports

The Business User benefits from Date Functions through Analytical Reporting tools built on Date-Function-enabled CDS views.

Instead of entering raw date values, users:

  • Select from a value help showing all available Date Functions with their human-readable names
  • Easily toggle between different Date Functions to shift the analysis window — for example, switching from CURRENT_MONTH to PREVIOUS_MONTH with a single selection
  • Get results that are always current and accurate, because the date range resolves at query execution time

This dramatically simplifies the reporting experience and reduces the chance of user error when specifying time periods.

 

4. ABAP Developer — Consume via API

For developers who need to use dynamic date logic in ABAP programs, reports, or classes, Date Functions are accessible via a C1-released CDS view API.

This API returns the resolved dynamic date or date range for a given Date Function — which the developer can then use directly in SELECT statements or any time-based processing logic.

 
 
" Example: Use the Date Function API to resolve a dynamic date range
" and filter records within that time frame
SELECT * FROM your_cds_view
  WHERE posting_date BETWEEN @LV_start_date AND @LV_end_date.

The value help integration also works in ABAP contexts, making it easy to discover and reference available Date Functions programmatically.

 

Coming soon: SAP is also working on enabling developers to define Date Functions directly within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) — using the same development environment as CDS views, with a form-based UI and simulate/test capabilities built in. This is currently in progress (WIP) and subject to change, but it will further unify the developer experience.

Why This Matters for Steampunk / BTP ABAP Cloud Environment

If you're building on the SAP BTP ABAP Environment (Steampunk), Date Functions are particularly valuable because:

  • They align with the clean core philosophy — time logic lives in a maintainable, transportable definition rather than scattered across code
  • They support extensibility without modification — key users can evolve date definitions independently of developer cycles
  • The CDS parameter approach keeps analytical views flexible and reusable across multiple time horizons
  • The upcoming ADT integration will make Date Functions a first-class citizen of the developer toolchain, consistent with how other CDS artefacts are managed

Summary

Persona Tool Capability
Key UserManage Date Functions appDefine, simulate, and transport Date Functions
DeveloperCDS ViewsDeclare Date Function parameters with defaults
Business UserAnalytical ReportsSelect Date Functions via value help at runtime
ABAP DeveloperABAP Development Tool Kit (ADT)Resolve dynamic dates for use in ABAP logic (WIP)

 

Coming soon: SAP is also working on enabling developers to define Date Functions directly within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) — using the same development environment as CDS views, with a form-based UI and simulate/test capabilities built in. This is currently in progress (WIP) and subject to change, but it will further unify the developer experience.

ABAP Developer Define and Consume Custom Date Function in ADT (Development User)

High-Impact Use Cases

  • Dynamic Financial Period Reporting: Instead of hard-coding dates, developers can use the PREVIOUSMONTH or Fiscal Year conversion reuse functions. This allows an analytical dashboard to automatically adjust its data range based on the system's current date without any code changes.
  • SLA and Deadline Tracking: Partners can leverage standardized functions like DATN_DAYS_BETWEEN or utcl_add_seconds to calculate service level agreement (SLA) breaches or invoice due dates in real-time. This ensures that "Time to Resolve" calculations are identical whether they run on-stack in S/4HANA or side-by-side on BTP.
  • Time-Zone Aware Global Operations: For apps serving users in different regions, reuse components like abap_system_timezone or utcl_to_local automatically handle daylight savings and regional conversions. This is critical for logistics or workforce management apps where a "Requested Delivery Date" must be accurately localized for warehouses worldwide.

Key Benefits of Adoption

  • Zero-Downtime Upgrades: Because these components are "Released APIs" (C1 contract), they are guaranteed to remain stable during automated SAP cloud upgrades.
  • Performance Optimization: Date Functions is significantly faster than fetching data into the application layer for manual date math.
  • Reduced Development Maintenance: By using Date Functions partners offload the maintenance.

 

 👉 Please let us know if you have any busines case / functional usecase in which you would like to use the Custom Date Function in ABAP Development Tool Kit (ADT) 

👉 Drop your experience or questions in the comments — always happy to discuss!

👉reach out to murthy.v@sap.com achim.clemens@sap.com nitin.padmanabhan@sap.com ashwin.k.s@sap.com 

👉Have you used Date Functions in your projects? Drop your experience or questions in the comments — always happy to discuss!

entry.title: RAP Recommendations
entry.content:
Sometimes, filling out forms is quite cumbersome. Like when you need to select the country you live in from a dropdown and scroll through all the countries, while you know that the application could have just preselected the country. Going further, would it not be great to write a description of where you are traveling and have recommendations for the city automatically generated, such as accommodations or restaurants?

To achieve this, RAP recommendations are available with SAP BTP ABAP environment 2602 and SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2602. RAP recommendations are an input assistance that proposes field values for input fields in a RAP application on an SAP Fiori UI. The proposed field values are based on the user input and the interaction history.

Motivation

Before the introduction of RAP recommendations, business users had to manually enter input into input fields or select values from a value help. The problem with that is:

  • Data entries may differ slightly from user to user.
  • Thinking about each entry is not efficient.
  • Finding the right terms is challenging.

By providing RAP recommendations, these difficulties are improved:

  • Data entries are consistent.
  • Business users' efficiency is increased by consistency and the proposed values.
  • The most likely value is predicted from the data context.

RAP recommendations enrich value helps with context-aware suggestions. Currently, there are two approaches to implement RAP recommendations: generative AI and deterministic rules.

Implementation

Before implementing RAP recommendations in your application, we need to look at the prerequisites and steps.

Prerequisite

RAP recommendations are built on top of a value help. Therefore, you need to have a value help implemented for the same field where you want to implement the RAP recommendation.

Steps

  1. Define RAP recommendations in the behavior definition.
  2. Implement RAP recommendations in the behavior pool of your RAP business object (using generative AI or deterministic rules).
  3. Define RAP recommendations in the projection behavior definition.

To understand RAP recommendations in detail, you can download the ABAP Flight Reference Scenario (GitHub) for RAP recommendations on GitHub. There, RAP recommendations are used in a travel example: After typing a description, generative AI suggests a destination, and then another RAP recommendation is triggered, suggesting accommodations using deterministic rules. Below is this example in a shorter form.

Example

This example shows the implementation of RAP recommendations using generative AI and deterministic rules. As mentioned above, you can download this example on GitHub. The following screenshot shows the RAP application requesting travel information.

RAP Recommendations

In the example, the business user selected Bulgaria as the destination. Right after the selection, three (fictive) accommodation recommendations for Sofia appear, and the business user can select the most fitting entry. Now, we will get through the implementation details step by step.

Behavior Definition

Define the field for which you want to provide RAP recommendations:

field ( recommendations ) Destination, Accommodation;

Here, the two fields Destination and Accommodation are specified to show the recommended values on the UI.

Define the function that runs the behavior:

recommendation function GetRecommendedValuesFunction;

Hint: Only one function can be specified for the recommended values.

Define side effects to specify which field change triggers the recommendation function:

side effects
  {
    recommendation function GetRecommendedValuesFunction
      executed on field Description, field Destination;
  }

The input for the recommended values is based on the two fields Description and Destination.

Behavior Pool

Define how the recommended values are returned:

METHOD GetRecommendedValuesFunction.
    READ ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_traveltp_rec IN LOCAL MODE
       ENTITY Travel
       FIELDS ( Description Destination ) WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
       RESULT DATA(business_user_travel)
       FAILED failed.

    "...

    LOOP AT business_user_travel ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<travel>).
      "...

      INSERT VALUE #(  %tky = <travel>-%tky
                       %param-travel = VALUE #( destination = CORRESPONDING #( destination_rec_values )
                                                accommodation = CORRESPONDING #( accommodation_rec_values ) ) ) INTO TABLE result.
    ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

The fields Description and Destination of the Travel entity are read and returned in the business_user_travel internal table. Inside the LOOP statement, the transactional key %tky and the result parameter %param for the component travel are filled.

You can find a description of the complete example in Example: Implementing RAP Recommendations (SAP Help Portal).

Deterministic Rules

For the deterministic rules approach, you can, for example, simply implement a query with a condition against your business user data. For example:

SELECT DISTINCT acc_name
          FROM /dmo/acc_rec_h AS accommodation
          INNER JOIN _user_travel AS destination
          ON accommodation~acc_country = destination~Destination
        INTO TABLE @result.

In this query, the Destination value is compared with the generated acc_country value.

Generative AI

To use the generative AI approach, you need to set up a scenario using the ABAP AI SDK (SAP Help Portal). In your Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) scenario, you can define prompt settings that are used to call the LLM Completion API. When the call is successful, the LLM's answer is returned. For example:

METHOD get_completion_ai.
    DATA(completion_api_instance) = get_completion_api_instance( ).

    IF completion_api_instance IS BOUND.
      TRY.
          DATA(completion_api_result) = completion_api_instance->execute_for_string( prompt_setting ).
        CATCH cx_aic_completion_api.
          RETURN result.
      ENDTRY.

      result = completion_api_result->get_completion( ).
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD get_completion_api_instance.
    TRY.
        result = cl_aic_islm_compl_api_factory=>get( )->create_instance( 'MyIntelligentScen' ).
      CATCH cx_aic_api_factory.
        RETURN result.
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.

You can find a description of the complete example in Example: Implementing Generative AI Features for RAP Recommendations (SAP Help Portal).

Recommended Values

The recommended values expect a certain set of parameters:

INSERT VALUE #( value = <rec_values>
                score = 100 - counter
                suggested = COND #( WHEN counter = 1
                                    THEN abap_true
                                    ELSE abap_false ) ) INTO TABLE result.

value is a mandatory parameter and contains the recommended value. score and suggested are optional parameters. Using score, you can order the recommended values in the value help. Using suggested, you can enter a recommended value directly into the input field.

Projection Behavior Definition

To reuse RAP recommendations in the projection behavior definition, you need to add the following:

use recommendations;
use side effects;

Resources

  • Documentation on the overall concept (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model | SAP Help Portal)
  • Documentation on the implementation (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model | SAP Help Portal)
  • Syntax Documentation (ABAP Keyword Documentation | SAP Help Portal)

entry.title: Solving the Multi-Approval Cause Problem in S/4HANA Cloud Sales Document Workflow
entry.content:
The Problem

If you've set up Flexible Workflow for Sales Documents on SAP S/4HANA Cloud following the Approval Workflows for Sales Documents documentation, you've likely hit this wall: a sales representative modifies a sales order, changing the payment terms, incoterms, and net amount in a single save. Three fields changed. Three potential approval triggers. But only one ApprovalReason is recorded on the document header.

Which one wins? Whichever BAdI logic evaluates last. The others are silently discarded.

The business consequences are real:

  • Lost visibility — approvers see "Payment Terms Changed" but have no idea the price also moved
  • Incorrect routing — different reasons may require different approvers; only one path is activated
  • Audit gaps — compliance cannot trace which changes actually triggered the workflow

How Standard APM Works (Quick Recap)

For those familiar with the Approval Workflows for Sales Documents, the approval flow works like this:

  1. A change is made to the sales order
  2. Cloud BAdI SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON evaluates the change and sets one approval reason code
  3. The system matches that reason to a Workflow Definition (configured in Manage Sales Document Workflows)
  4. A single workflow instance is triggered

The BAdI is the only extension point for determining why approval is needed. But it outputs a single value. If your business has multiple independent approval triggers — price thresholds, incoterms changes, credit checks — you're forced to pick one or create composite reason codes that quickly become unmanageable.

Solution Architecture

The approach separates cause determination (what triggered approval) from reason assignment (what the workflow sees), using the existing BAdI execution sequence:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              BAdI Execution Sequence                         │
│                                                             │
│  1. SD_SLS_MODIFY_HEAD  → Generate UUID (stable key)       │
│  2. SD_SLS_FINALIZE     → Determine ALL causes,            │
│                            write to table + buffer,         │
│                            display on SO header             │
│  3. SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL → Read causes from buffer,         │
│       _REASON             set single reason for framework  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The key components:

  • Custom table (ztb_sd_apm_cause) — stores every triggered cause independently, keyed by a UUID
  • Helper class (zcl_sd_apm_cause_writer) — manages an in-memory buffer and DB persistence
  • Display field on the SO header (Key User Extensibility) — shows all causes concatenated (e.g., PAYMENT_TERMS, INCOTERMS, NET_AMOUNT)

The Two-Tier Extensibility Model

This solution bridges Developer Extensibility and Key User Extensibility — a pattern that's essential on S/4HANA Cloud but not always well understood:

Layer What's Created Why

Developer Extensibility (ABAP Cloud, ADT)Custom table ztb_sd_apm_cause, helper class zcl_sd_apm_cause_writerThese are ABAP repository objects that require a developer environment and transport
Release for Key UserTable and class released with C1 contract (USE_IN_KEY_USER_APPS)Cloud BAdIs run in the Key User extensibility layer — they can only call released APIs
Key User Extensibility (Custom Logic app)BAdI implementations for SD_SLS_MODIFY_HEAD, SD_SLS_FINALIZE, SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASONThese are maintained in the Custom Logic app by functional consultants

The critical step: after creating the table and class via Developer Extensibility, you must release them with the appropriate contract so they become callable from Cloud BAdI implementations. Without this release, the Key User BAdI code cannot reference your custom objects — the syntax check will reject them.

See Developing an Extension Using Key User Extensibility for details on the release process.

Why UUID? The Late-Numbering Problem

During sales order creation, the SO number doesn't exist yet when the FINALIZE and APM BAdIs execute (late numbering). We need a stable identifier to link cause records to the order before the number is assigned.

The solution: generate a UUID in MODIFY_HEAD (the earliest BAdI that can write to SO custom fields), store it on the order header, and use it as the foreign key for cause records. Once the SO is saved, both the UUID and the SO number are available for later queries.

Key Design Decisions

Decision Rationale

Separate table instead of SO custom fieldsThe APM BAdI has a read-only contract — it cannot modify SO data. Causes must be stored externally.
UUID as primary key (not SO number)SO number not assigned during creation. UUID is available immediately.
UUID generated in MODIFY_HEADEarliest BAdI in the chain that can write to custom fields.
In-memory buffer in the helper classFINALIZE executes before the APM BAdI within the same LUW. The buffer bridges this gap without requiring a COMMIT.
Cause determination in FINALIZE onlySingle source of truth. The APM BAdI only reads causes and translates to the single reason the framework requires.
Developer Extensibility for table + classCloud BAdI implementations (Key User layer) cannot create tables or complex classes — only call released ones.

Each decision was driven by the BAdI execution order, the read/write contracts of each extension point, and the S/4HANA Cloud extensibility boundaries.

Implementation Highlights

1. Custom Table (Developer Extensibility)

The persistence layer — a simple transparent table keyed by UUID and cause code:

@EndUserText.label : 'Root Causes of Sales Document Approval Reason'
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table ztb_sd_apm_cause {
  key client              : abap.clnt not null;
  key sales_document      : abap.char(10) not null;
  key sales_document_uuid : abap.char(32) not null;
  key cause_code          : abap.char(30) not null;
  created_by              : abap.char(12);
}

2. Helper Class (Developer Extensibility, Released for Key User)

The class manages both an in-memory buffer (for same-LUW access between BAdIs) and database persistence. It's created in ADT and released with C1 - USE_IN_KEY_USER_APPS so Cloud BAdI implementations can call it.

CLASS zcl_sd_apm_cause_writer DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_cause,
             cause_code TYPE c LENGTH 30,
           END OF ty_cause.
    TYPES tt_causes TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_cause WITH EMPTY KEY.

    "! Write causes to buffer + DB. Called from FINALIZE BAdI.
    CLASS-METHODS write
      IMPORTING iv_sales_document_uuid TYPE sysuuid_c32
                iv_sales_document      TYPE vbeln_va
                it_causes              TYPE tt_causes.

    "! Read causes — buffer first (same LUW), DB fallback.
    "! Called from APM BAdI.
    CLASS-METHODS read
      IMPORTING iv_sales_document_uuid TYPE sysuuid_c32
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_causes)       TYPE tt_causes.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_buffer_entry,
             sales_document_uuid TYPE c LENGTH 32,
             cause_code          TYPE c LENGTH 30,
           END OF ty_buffer_entry.
    CLASS-DATA gt_buffer TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_buffer_entry WITH EMPTY KEY.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS zcl_sd_apm_cause_writer IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read.
    " Priority 1: in-memory buffer (bridges FINALIZE → APM within same LUW)
    rt_causes = VALUE #(
      FOR ls IN gt_buffer
      WHERE ( sales_document_uuid = iv_sales_document_uuid )
      ( cause_code = ls-cause_code ) ).

    IF rt_causes IS NOT INITIAL.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    " Priority 2: database (for reads after COMMIT, e.g. workflow display)
    SELECT cause_code FROM ztb_sd_apm_cause
      WHERE sales_document_uuid = @iv_sales_document_uuid
      INTO TABLE @rt_causes.
  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD write.
    " Refresh buffer for this document
    DELETE gt_buffer WHERE sales_document_uuid = iv_sales_document_uuid.
    LOOP AT it_causes INTO DATA(ls_cause).
      APPEND VALUE #( sales_document_uuid = iv_sales_document_uuid
                      cause_code          = ls_cause-cause_code ) TO gt_buffer.
    ENDLOOP.

    " Persist to DB (replace pattern: delete old, insert new)
    DELETE FROM ztb_sd_apm_cause
      WHERE sales_document_uuid = @iv_sales_document_uuid.

    IF it_causes IS INITIAL.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    INSERT ztb_sd_apm_cause FROM TABLE @( VALUE #(
      FOR ls IN it_causes
      ( sales_document      = iv_sales_document
        sales_document_uuid = iv_sales_document_uuid
        cause_code          = ls-cause_code
        created_by          = cl_abap_context_info=>get_user_technical_name( ) ) ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

3. BAdI: SD_SLS_MODIFY_HEAD — Generate UUID (Custom Logic App)

The earliest extension point that can write to custom fields. Generates a UUID once per document lifecycle:

" Generate stable UUID for linking cause records to this sales order.
" Required because SO number is not yet assigned during creation (late numbering).
IF salesdocument-yyy1904248uuid_sdh IS INITIAL.
  salesdocument_change-yyy1904248uuid_sdh = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c32_static( ).
  salesdocument_change-control-yyy1904248uuid_sdh = 'X'.
ENDIF.

4. BAdI: SD_SLS_FINALIZE — Determine Causes + Display (Custom Logic App)

The core logic: evaluate which fields changed and persist all triggered causes. Uses the salesdocument_before_image parameter to detect actual changes (not just current values):

DATA lt_causes TYPE zcl_sd_apm_cause_writer=>tt_causes.

" --- Cause: Payment Terms changed to a monitored value ---
IF salesdocument-customerpaymentterms <> salesdocument_before_image-customerpaymentterms
  AND salesdocument-customerpaymentterms = '0007'.
  APPEND VALUE #( cause_code = 'PAYMENT_TERMS' ) TO lt_causes.
ENDIF.

" --- Cause: Incoterms changed ---
IF salesdocument-incotermsclassification <> salesdocument_before_image-incotermsclassification.
  APPEND VALUE #( cause_code = 'INCOTERMS' ) TO lt_causes.
ENDIF.

" --- Cause: Net amount exceeds threshold ---
IF salesdocument-totalnetamount > 10000
  AND salesdocument_before_image-totalnetamount <= 10000.
  APPEND VALUE #( cause_code = 'NET_AMOUNT_THRESHOLD' ) TO lt_causes.
ENDIF.

" Write all causes to buffer + DB
zcl_sd_apm_cause_writer=>write(
  iv_sales_document_uuid = salesdocument-yyy1904248uuid_sdh
  iv_sales_document      = salesdocument-salesdocument
  it_causes              = lt_causes ).

" Display concatenated causes on SO header for approver visibility
IF lt_causes IS NOT INITIAL.
  salesdocument_change-yyy1904248approvalreas_sdh = REDUCE string(
    INIT result = ``
    FOR ls IN lt_causes
    NEXT result = COND #(
      WHEN result IS INITIAL THEN ls-cause_code
      ELSE result && `, ` && ls-cause_code ) ).
ELSE.
  salesdocument_change-yyy1904248approvalreas_sdh = ''.
ENDIF.
salesdocument_change-control-yyy1904248approvalreas_sdh = 'X'.

5. BAdI: SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON — Read + Route (Custom Logic App)

The APM BAdI reads the causes from the buffer and sets the single reason code the framework requires. The routing logic remains simple — the cause table provides the intelligence:

" Read all causes determined in FINALIZE (from buffer, same LUW)
DATA(lt_causes) = zcl_sd_apm_cause_writer=>read(
  salesdocument-yyy1904248uuid_sdh ).

" If any cause was triggered, set approval reason to activate the workflow
IF lt_causes IS NOT INITIAL.
  salesdocapprovalreason = 'ZOR1'.
ENDIF.

" Future enhancement: map specific causes to different reason codes
" for routing to different workflow definitions per cause type.

The end-to-end flow: UUID is generated → FINALIZE detects changes and writes causes → APM BAdI reads causes and triggers the workflow. The approver sees all causes on the order header immediately.

What This Can and Cannot Achieve

Let me be direct about the boundaries.

What works within standard Flexible Workflow:

  • Full visibility of all approval triggers on the sales order
  • Data foundation for multi-level sequential approval — use the Workflow Condition BAdI to dynamically skip or require each step based on the cause table
  • Complete audit trail of what actually triggered approval

What this does NOT deliver:

  • True parallel approval — One SO = one workflow instance. The framework has no fork/join construct. You cannot spawn parallel approval paths for the same document.
  • Multiple simultaneous workflow definitions — Routing is based on one reason → one workflow definition. There's no mechanism to activate multiple definitions simultaneously.

This is not a limitation of the implementation. It's inherent to the S/4HANA Cloud Flexible Workflow engine. The "Manage Sales Document Workflow" app supports only sequential steps (Step 1 → Step 2 → ...).

Future Directions

The cause table establishes a data foundation that both internal and external routing can consume:

  1. Workflow Condition BAdI — query the cause table per step to dynamically decide whether a specific approver is needed. Step 1 handles price changes, Step 2 handles incoterms, Step 3 handles payment terms. Each step checks if its cause is present and skips if not.

  2. SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) — for organizations that need true parallel approval, BPA with Event Mesh can subscribe to the order change event and branch into parallel paths. The cause table provides exactly the routing data BPA needs to decide which branches to activate.

  3. Configuration-driven cause definitions — replace hardcoded checks with a configuration table mapping field changes to cause codes. This enables no-code extensibility for new approval triggers.

The value of building the data layer first: when the framework evolves (or you integrate BPA), the cause data is already there. No rework required.

Conclusion

When your business has multiple independent reasons to approve a sales order, a single ApprovalReason field isn't enough. This approach works within the BAdI execution sequence and the two-tier extensibility model (Developer + Key User) to capture what the standard field cannot, while providing immediate approver visibility.

The total implementation footprint is small: one transparent table, one helper class (both via Developer Extensibility, released for Key User consumption), and three Cloud BAdI implementations. All in ABAP Cloud, all within the S/4HANA Cloud extensibility model.

Prerequisites: This post assumes familiarity with Flexible Workflow setup on S/4HANA Cloud. If you're starting from scratch, see Approval Workflows for Sales Documents and Custom Fields App and Custom Logic App in SAP Help.

entry.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Upport and Downport in SAP Using TCode UDO
entry.content:
Introduction:-

How I Use TCode UDO to Handle Downporting in SAP — A Practical Walkthrough:

I've spent a fair amount of time dealing with transport headaches in SAP landscapes where teams run parallel releases. One of the tools that's genuinely made my life easier in those situations is TCode UDO — the Upport/Downport Overview. I want to share how I actually use it, because the few times I went looking for a practical guide, I couldn't find one that covered the full flow from setup to verification.

Why I Needed UDO in the First Place:

The problem I kept running into: we'd develop a fix or a new function in a higher-release system, and then need to get it into a lower-release landscape for customers still on that version. The usual go-to tools — SCWB and SNOTE — handle code objects fine, but they fall short when your change involves DDICs, documentation, message classes, or table contents. I learned this the hard way after a failed downport left a data element missing in the target system and broke an F1 help link in production.

That's exactly the gap UDO fills. It generates a dedicated ABAP report (named UDO_NOTE_<SAPNoteNumber>, or RS_UDO_NOTE_<X> in BW) that gets packaged into a deliverable and shipped inside an SAP Note. When a customer applies that note via SNOTE, the report runs and handles all the DDIC and documentation creation automatically. Clean, auditable, and no manual recreation needed.
Here's a complete guide to SAP TCode UDO (Upport Downport Overview). Let's start with the landscape architecture — what the tool does at a system level.

Now here's the step-by-step interactive walkthrough of the exact actions inside TCode UDO.
The One Pre-Requisite That Catches People Out:

Before you even open UDO, you need an RFC connection between your source and target systems — and it has to be a Dialog User type connection, not a Background User. This is the thing that trips up new people most often.

If the connection type is wrong, UDO will fail silently or give confusing errors when it tries to compare object versions remotely. I always ask Basis to double-check this upfront. In SM59, the connection should look roughly like:

  • Connection type: 3 (ABAP connection)
  • Logon type: Dialog user ← this is the one that matters
  • Target host and system number pointing to the source system

Open TCode UDO in the target system

Log into the target system (the lower-release system you want to downport into). Open transaction UDO.

UDO — Upport Downport Overview (initial screen) 

Transport Request(s)           DEV K900123 ← TR from source
Source system                       DEV (client 100)
RFC destination                   SRCSYS_100
Only documentation            [ ] (unchecked = all objects)

You enter the TR number from the source system, but you run UDO from inside the target system. The RFC connection fetches the version comparison remotely.

Review the object list

UDO fetches all objects from the TR and displays them with their status in both systems. Each row shows whether the object exists locally and what copy mechanism will be used.

UDO — Object overview list 

Object                              Type                            Local                      Copy via
_______________________________________________________________

ZMAT_TAB                   TABD(table)                older                       UDO
ZMAT_PROG                PROG(report)             older                      SCWB
ZMAT_DE                      DTED(data el.)           missing                   UDO
ZMAT_DOC                   DOCU (F1 help)         missing                   UDO

Use the glasses icon to preview the object in the target, and the binoculars icon to preview in the source. The "Copy via" column shows whether UDO or SCWB handles the object — this is automatic.

Select objects and run simulation 

Select the rows you want to copy (or select all), then click the Copy Selected Objects button in the toolbar. UDO first runs a simulation (test run) before making any changes.

Simulation result — no actual changes yet 

Object                                Sim. status
________________________________________

ZMAT_TAB                       OK — will update
ZMAT_PROG                    OK — will copy
